description:
You are a technical writer who keeps all work-in-progress documentation under
    /home/user/docs_work.  
    You would like to keep a second, up-to-date copy of that directory in
    /home/user/remote_backup (pretend this is an SSH-mounted location even
    though it is just another local directory for the purpose of this
    exercise).  The goal is to automate the synchronisation process and
    generate a machine-verifiable report every time a sync is performed.

    REQUIRED END-STATE
    1. The directory /home/user/docs_work must exist and contain **exactly**
       the three items listed below (create them if they are not already
       present).  
         • /home/user/docs_work/introduction.md  
           Content (including the terminating newline):
           --------------------------------------------------
           # Introduction
           This documentation repository contains all introductory materials.
           --------------------------------------------------

         • /home/user/docs_work/user_guide.md  
           Content (including the terminating newline):
           --------------------------------------------------
           # User Guide
           Follow these instructions carefully to operate the system.
           --------------------------------------------------

         • /home/user/docs_work/images/logo.png  
           (Create the directory “images” if necessary.)
           Content (including the terminating newline):
           --------------------------------------------------
           i am not a real png file, but it stands in for one during testing.
           --------------------------------------------------

    2. Create the directory /home/user/remote_backup.  After synchronisation it
       must contain an exact, one-to-one mirror of /home/user/docs_work
       (directory layout, file names, file contents, modification times, and
       permissions).

    3. Write a synchronisation script at
       /home/user/scripts/sync_docs.sh with these mandatory properties:
         • Uses rsync (or an equivalent standard tool that preserves
           permissions, timestamps, directory structure, and deletes files
           removed from the source) with the –av --delete semantics.
         • Takes no arguments – source and destination paths are hard-coded to
           /home/user/docs_work and /home/user/remote_backup.
         • Writes a one-line, CSV-formatted report for every regular file that
           is transferred (either created or updated) to
           /home/user/sync_logs/sync_status.log.  The required CSV columns are:
             1. RelativePath – path relative to /home/user/docs_work
             2. SizeBytes   – exact byte size of the file *after* transfer
             3. Sha256      – lowercase hexadecimal SHA-256 checksum of the file
           Example line (values will of course differ):
             introduction.md,83,19a3e6de9d5c6be949…
         • Appends to the log file if it already exists (do NOT overwrite).

       A typical successful run that copied two files and updated one would make
       the log file look exactly like this (three separate lines shown):
           introduction.md,83,19a3e6de9d5c6be949…
           user_guide.md,78,b17999ce73d0ee598d7…
           images/logo.png,60,3a309c4e0bbce5ac360…

       NOTE – the testing harness will verify:
         • That /home/user/scripts/sync_docs.sh is present and executable
           (chmod +x).
         • That /home/user/remote_backup is an exact mirror of
           /home/user/docs_work.
         • That /home/user/sync_logs/sync_status.log exists, contains one CSV
           row for *each* regular file currently inside /home/user/docs_work,
           and that each row’s size and SHA-256 values are correct.  Order of
           rows is not important, but all three files must appear exactly once.

    4. You must actually execute the script at least once so the first sync and
       corresponding log entries are produced before the task is finished.

    5. No root privileges may be assumed at any point.  All paths are under
       /home/user, which is writable by the current user.

    6. When you are done, the following files and directories must exist:
         • /home/user/docs_work  (with its three files)
         • /home/user/remote_backup (mirror copy)
         • /home/user/scripts/sync_docs.sh  (executable)
         • /home/user/sync_logs/sync_status.log (CSV log)

    7. Use only standard POSIX command-line tools shipped with a vanilla Linux
       distribution (bash, rsync, mkdir, sha256sum, etc.).

    8. The terminal output produced while working is not strictly checked, but
       try to include informative echo statements so a human observer can tell
       what is happening.

    This concludes the specification.  Implement the above items in the
    terminal.  (You are *not* supposed to paste the commands here; interact in
    the shell.)
truth:
Initial filesystem state before the task starts for automated testing:
      • Only an empty home directory exists: /home/user
      • Directories /home/user/scripts, /home/user/sync_logs, /home/user/docs_work,
        and /home/user/remote_backup do NOT yet exist.

    After the agent completes the task the tester will assert:
      1. /home/user/docs_work/introduction.md exists and its byte-for-byte
         contents equal exactly:
         "# Introduction\nThis documentation repository contains all introductory materials.\n"

      2. /home/user/docs_work/user_guide.md exists and its byte-for-byte
         contents equal exactly:
         "# User Guide\nFollow these instructions carefully to operate the system.\n"

      3. /home/user/docs_work/images/logo.png exists and its byte-for-byte
         contents equal exactly:
         "i am not a real png file, but it stands in for one during testing.\n"

      4. /home/user/remote_backup is an exact mirror of /home/user/docs_work:
           • Same three files
           • Same directory hierarchy
           • Each mirrored file’s SHA-256 checksum matches the source

      5. /home/user/scripts/sync_docs.sh exists and is executable.  It must
         contain an rsync command with the flags “-av” and “--delete” applied to
         the source /home/user/docs_work and the destination
         /home/user/remote_backup.  The script must also compute SHA-256
         checksums (by calling `sha256sum` or equivalent) and append properly
         formatted CSV lines to /home/user/sync_logs/sync_status.log.

      6. /home/user/sync_logs/sync_status.log is present and:
           • Contains exactly three lines (one per file)
           • Each line matches the regex
             ^(introduction\.md|user_guide\.md|images/logo\.png),[0-9]+,[0-9a-f]{64}$
           • For every path listed, the numeric size equals the actual file size
             reported by `stat -c%s`, and the hash is the true SHA-256 checksum
             of the corresponding file in /home/user/remote_backup.

    Any deviation from these requirements constitutes a failure.
name: task_000000_ab245975
